free_text: A search for an excited state of B9 near 1.7 MeV
Livermore 230 cm variable energy
cyclotron.
BP
A thin self-supported polystyrene (CH) target located
at the center of a 1 m diam. scattering chamber.
Targets of thicknesses ranging from 50 to
150 microg/cm2 were used.
The alpha spectra were measured using a thin
Ortec Surface Barrier detector (50 microm thickness,
totally depleted at a reverse bias of about 100 V).
The absolute differential cross sections
for the ground state group are accurate to within
about 25%. The large uncertainty is due to the fact
that the usual precautions with regard to charge
collection have not been carefully observed since the
main emphasis in this experiment was on energy
spectra. In those cases where the continuous spectra
are well described by the four-body shape, the
breakup cross section is accurate to within 25 %.
Fig. 1, page 121.

Fig. 4, page 123.
A 1.28 mg/cm2 Be target evaporated on a 0.5 mm thick
tantalum plate which was thick enough to stop the
proton beam.
The neutron detectors, consisting of 5 x 5 cm
plastic scintillators mounted on RCA-6655
photomultipliers, were positioned, at 15 degree
angular intervals in a well collimated and shielded
geometry.
The neutron spectra were observed using time-of-
flight techniques. A flight-path of about 10 m was
chosen as an optimum considering neutron intensities
and resolution of the experimental equipment.
The absolute differential cross sections
for the ground state group are accurate to within
about 25%. The large uncertainty is due to the fact
that the usual precautions with regard to charge
collection have not been carefully observed since the
main emphasis in this experiment was on energy
spectra. In those cases where the continuous spectra
are well described by the four-body shape, the
breakup cross section is accurate to within 25 %.
